speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. Today's date is August the 16th, 1996, and the survivor's name is Jack Gun. So you were getting to the point of where you're coming to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Maybe I-- I should have said, I told you that-- I told you, my brother found some addresses and pictures. And-- on the address-- one of the addresses he had was from a man that I had in Pennsylvania. I didn't have the-- he knew the-- had the name of the town, and-- and their last name. And it happened to have been a very small town in Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- in the DP camp, we were on the American zone, so there was American soldiers, naturally. And one was from Pittsburgh, and my brother gave him the letter. When he went home, and he mailed it in Pittsburgh, and-- and it came to my uncle who lived in Brackenridge, Pennsylvania, which was a very small town. Just a little bit bigger than Rozyszcze, I believe. And they-- they were a prominent family there, so they got the letter, and they got in contact with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-in-law also had-- maybe let-- let me backtrack a little. In '46-- And in January, '46, my brother married. That's when he got married. There was a rabbi there, and they took out the [? ketubah, ?] and-- and [? raised ?] [? civil, ?] you know, certificate, and he got married there. And they told my sister-in-law now you inherit an 11-year-old or 12-year-- almost 12-year-old son. Because, you know, she knew that I would live with them, and that she would have to take care of me. And she happened to be a extraordinary person. I mean, to put up with what she did, and she happens to be very special to me. Now-- So she also-- She had two uncles living in Detroit, and they sent an affidavit, and we also got one from my aunt and uncle from Pennsylvania. And in November of 1947, we started the process-- We finally-- Our quota came up and we started the process to go to the United States. And we went from Linz to Munich, and we stayed there for a little bit of time. And from there, we went to the council, had to take physicals and all kinds of stuff. And-- And finally, we went to [? Bremerhaven, ?] Germany. From there, we went on a boat. And we arrived to the United States on January the 29th, 1948. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we had second cousins in New York that met us and took us to their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What'd it feel like when you first came here to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: To New York? I felt, again, like a stranger in paradise, you know? Something new, something different, you know? And in such a short time in my-- from my young years or from my youth here, I was already so many different surroundings, so many different things. I was probably confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you end up in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came, which we were-- I told you the cousins of ours picked us up in New York. We had dinner there. And I think spent a couple of days with them. They-- In fact, I remember they went out and bought me a suit, and bought my brother something. And-- And then my sister-in-law's cousin came from Detroit, and brought us to Detroit to see their family. We spent here, in Detroit, a few days. Then we went to my family in Pennsylvania, in Brackenridge, all within a-- you know, a couple of weeks' time. We went to Brackenridge, and we met my aunt, and-- which was my father's sister, and my uncle. And he had three sons and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my aunt told my brother, she says, you know, Anszel, she says, you're-- you just came here, and you have no job, you have no money. You have no apartment. You don't have anything. Why don't you leave [? Yankele ?] with us till you get situated, you know, till you get a job, till you earn a little money or whatever? And my brother thought it over, and he thought, hey, you know, it's not such a bad idea, you know? Because he-- my sister-in-law's uncle had an extra bedroom, so he offered-- they could stay with him for a while. So this was sort of, you know, it-- it felt that it would be good for both, you know? And I remained there for four years. I lived with them from '48, approximately February, till-- till June of '52. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, again, I felt [SIGHS] funny. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I was used to my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And here, I'm alone again, but I got used to it. And I went to school there, and also worked. And they had this little supermarket and a slaughterhouse. And-- And I-- you know, used to go to school, and work there in the afternoon, and sometimes late in the night. Just depends, you know, how much work there had to be done. And I used to call my brother so often and-- Well, not that often. You know, those days, long distance calls was like today, yeah, calling Mars. So I used to call him maybe once a month, but we used to write letters to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to go in the summertime to visit them. And I-- and I know in '49, sometimes, he had a very hard time there, you know? He was working for Chrysler, and he was laid off, and he just had his first daughter, was born in March of '49. And he was having a difficult time. So I used to tell my uncle and aunt, I says, you know, I says, you know, you should get little money together, and send to my brother. I says, you know, he's having a hard time right now. So they did. And I got accustomed to living there, I guess, you know? And they had-- They-- My aunt-- My aunt had grandsons approximately my age. Some a little older, so, you know, I-- So what-- what occupation did you end up doing for--

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the majority of your life? What did you end up being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. What did I end up being? Well, I guess, maybe because of being there, I got-- I got involved in the meat business. And I-- I worked in a supermarket here, and-- Oh, well, when I-- while I was there, naturally, I was still young, and I stayed there-- I told you I stayed there till '52. In '51, my aunt passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother came, naturally, to the funeral, and he wanted-- and then he figured it's enough. He was already working, and making a decent wage, and had an apartment. So he wanted me to come to live back with him. So my uncle, all of a sudden, he wasn't that-- a sympathetic man. But all of a sudden, he became very emotional, and he says, look, now I need him. He says, my wife died, and I'm all alone. And he says, now, you're gonna take him away from me. So my brother talked to me, and he says, what do you think? And I says, well, I said, I don't know. I said, let me try maybe another year, if I can make it here. Well, my-- my uncle was one of these-- happened to have been very religious, and a disciplinarian, you know? He was very strict. Never had too many good things to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- But I guess I got used to him already, and so I stayed for another year. And then, within that year, you know, he was religious. He can't be without a wife, so he remarried already. And in-- when I finished school in June, I-- my brother came there and we decided that's enough, and I came to Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you end up meeting your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I meet my wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Miryam. Her maiden name was Goldstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met at the Jewish Center at a dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandra is the oldest daughter. She's 35. And Sam is my son. He's 33.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has a son Matthew, which is six, and a daughter, Laurel, which is almost three. And my son has two boys, Max, which is almost three, and Joshua, which is six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Anszel living now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anszel now lives in Florida. When he retired, he moved out to Florida, lives permanently in Boca Raton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever gone back to your hometown recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1992, we went back to Rozyszcze. We have some [? Lunz ?] people that are from our town that live in Israel. And they previously went back there and arranged with the mayor of the town to build a monument. And in July of 1992, we went back there. Myself, my brother, and his two children, his two oldest children, a son and a daughter went, and we had a big ceremony. The-- The ravine and the-- the-- the field that the-- our-- that the-- that all the people from the ghetto were killed was still there, empty. Just an empty field. And this is where we put on a big monument which I'll show you in a picture. And it was a very, very emotional day for me. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] But we-- we did whatever we could. We said kaddish, and that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- How long did you go for? Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were just there a couple days. And my-- And my brother's son, well, happens to be very emotional fellow, and he was-- he didn't stop cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been involved in other Holocaust-related activities, organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm involved with the Hidden Children of Michigan. We meet every four to six weeks, and [? met ?] in somebody's house. And this gives a chance of some of the people that cannot talk, or have a hard time talking. They tell their stories, and you know, they-- they find a little bit of satisfaction out of it, you know? They-- They-- That's the only place where they feel comfortable to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken in schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have spoken to-- in-- I speak mainly here at the Holocaust Center when they bring groups of children in. And I tell them my story. I've been [? talking ?] [? in ?] community college and spoke to a class which was studying the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that experience like, talking to the kids about your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just started it recently, a little over a year maybe. I have a hard time talking, you know? But I do it mainly for the-- because I feel it's my duty to do it. And now, where there's so few of us left, and I'm one of the-- I'm one of the babies of the Holocaust, and I'm 62. So I feel it's part of my duty to-- for future generations to know, and that's the reason I'm doing it

speaker: interviewer
text: They say that there are some people that have to grow up very, very young. And they sort of don't have a childhood. Do you-- Do you feel that same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I grew up too fast, too soon. I had no childhood. At 10 years old I was-- I was a businessman going to-- to sell yeast. I didn't know what it is to play, to ride a bicycle. This is the main-- this is my main problem, probably, that I grew up so fast, without enjoying what normal kids enjoy at my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you see-- when you had-- your kids were growing up, and-- and your grandchildren, do those thoughts ever come back, about the fact that they're going through things that you weren't able to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. And there was many times when-- when my son was eight years old, I thought to myself, I says, my gosh, I says, look. I-- I couldn't picture how he would react in my situation. Or how he-- It's-- It's even unthinkable of any American or not only American, or any nation of-- a-- a-- a kid that age that lives under normal circumstances to go through what I went through, to be without parents at the age of eight, and to be in hiding, and know not to-- not to holler-- and know not to make any noise. Just, I-- a lot of times I wondered how this-- It's-- It's like a dream. A lot of times, I don't believe myself that I went through it. How?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said before that one of the things that you would do was to shut your emotions off, or not show your emotions.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has that changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely changed, the-- the-- with age, or maturity, I guess. And now, I happen to be very emotional, and anything, you know, I'd see about the Holocaust or any tragedy, naturally, I-- I'm easy-- easy-- easy to cry. I guess I'm letting out the tears that I kept back before. When I was young, I just-- I never wanted to talk about it much at all. Even my children, my son, especially, is very-- was very, very inquisitive, not that my daughter didn't want to know, but she's just a different type. But he was always one of these types that's-- wants to know everything, and how, and when, and where. And when he was young, I had a hard time telling him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to tell him, I says, you know, why don't you ask your Uncle Anszel? He's much older. He remembers more. I-- I tried not to talk. Maybe, I don't know, it was hard for me to talk about it. Maybe I didn't want them to be sad, or make him feel bad for me, but I could not speak. And people used to ask me, when I was in Pennsylvania, I was in school, and a lot of kids, hey, you went through the war, right? And I was, yeah, I went through the war. And you know, just brush it off, put it aside. Did-- Did you find, when you came to the United States, did-- was-- were there any people who wanted to know about the war, or did you talk about it to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They wanted to know. A lot of people asked. And then, you know, and then also, we-- especially the people that were hidden, or hidden children, had a little bit an inferiority complex, I think. Because everybody used to talk about concentration camps, you know, Auschwitz, Mauthausen, Bergen-Belsen, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we felt, jeez, we didn't go through anything, you know? And that, jeez, these people, they had numbers, and what they went through, and you know? So we felt like the-- don't know what word to use. Luckier than-- than those who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not luckier. Like, we're in the-- We're in the background, you know? We're not-- we're not on the main arena from the Holocaust survivors. Like, we were in-- But from what I understand, is-- I mean, from a-- I heard some historians claim that maybe the people hiding might have had more emotional problems than than concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the ones in the concentration camps, the ones that they didn't kill, the ones that survived and were just working, they had more of a normal-- they got used to a normal way of life, you know? Not that it was a good way of life, but they had a certain routine. While us, by hiding, we were constantly, constantly in that fear, and afraid, and always afraid, here, this one's-- they're going to catch me here.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're going to catch me here. Any little sound, any little noise, any little, you know, when we heard a dog barking. We said, oh, here comes the dog to get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you find that there are-- are any triggers that set off memories nowadays? Are-- are there-- Is there a fear of certain places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think this is one of my-- one of my things that I have-- that remained with me mainly, is fear. I'm very-- Up until today, I'm scared to sleep at home alone, I'm ashamed to say, and I'm 62 years old. And I don't-- I avoid to sleep home alone. And I'm sure that's left from the hiding in the war. Also I'm very claustrophobic. I'm afraid to be any place enclosed. I get-- We went someplace on a trip, and we went into caves, you know, low down and-- in fact, it was on the same time that I went with my brother to Rozyszcze. We stopped in Kiev, we went from Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while we were in Kiev, we were sightseeing and we went into this one cave. And it was so low, and, you know, very-- very enclosed. And I got a-- a-- a cold sweat on me. And my brother, and he says, what's the matter with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I just can't be here. I says, it's not for me. I'm very, very-- I cannot be in any enclosed places. I'm-- I also realize that some people that never went through what I did are also claustrophobic, but in my particular case, I feel that's strictly from being in a-- hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you deal with those fears?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best I can. I tried to avoid, as much as possible. Even in an elevator, when I go someplace, I usually try to look to see if there's somebody else around getting on there. But if I have to, I do go on an elevator myself, but I'm scared. My heart shakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any-- any message that you would like to leave for anybody who is watching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who sees this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, mainly, I want-- I want the people in-- in-- you know, especially, maybe not-- even non-Jews to-- to be aware of the tragedies that can come from hatred and racism. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to make sure to see that they don't let this new wave of skinheads and white supremacists claim that-- that there was never a Holocaust, and to say that they want to finish what Hitler didn't. And I hope and pray that people in general will be more aware of things-- of-- of the Holocaust, and to see that something like this never happens again to anybody, anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any final thoughts or comments you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly what else, unless you want to ask me something that I-- you think that I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you said it all. I really do. Thank you very much for your testimony. OK. And what's this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my grandfather on the left with the beard, and in the center is my father, and on the right side is a cousin of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people? There was my-- I believe that was my father's father. His name was Yaakov Gun. I was one named after, and [? Schmuel ?] Gun, my father, and this cousin was Mendel Dichter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. But it looks like when my father must have been in his 20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my father, [? Schmuel, ?] and this is myself. I think maybe I was four years old. So would probably be 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this one of the pictures that was recovered--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the-- from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of the pictures my-- my brother found in the attic, I believe. And this is the backyard--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Sonia and my father, [? Schmuel. ?] And in the center is my grandmother, Miriam, which is my mother's mother. And on the left is my sister, Raizel, and my brother, Anszel, on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wasn't born then. This picture must have been taken, oh, I would say it looks like approximately-- my brother looks there about four years old, wouldn't you say? So probably 1928, '29. All right. What's this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- on the left is my brother, Anszel, and my mother next to him, and I'm in the middle, and my father on the far right. I have no idea why my sister wasn't present at the time, but this is what we found. And this is in our home town, Rozyszcze, probably approximately 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my brother, far left, my sister-in-law Manya next to him. Next to her is my first cousin, [? Shika ?] Dichter and his wife, Malka. And I'm in the center. This was approximately 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken in DP camp in Linz, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself on the certificate of-- of a passport, getting ready to come to the United States. It was taken in 1947. It was taken probably in Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- was taken when I came to the United States in-- in Brackenridge, Pennsylvania, and was put into the newspaper. As you can see the title there, it says, 'He's Mere 15 and Has Lived 1,000 Years.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. Oh. OK, what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in July, 1992, when we went back to put a monument in Rozyszcze for all the people that were killed. And the second from the left is my brother, Anszel. I'm next to him. And just a little bit above my left in my niece, Binnie. And way on top, the third one from the left, is my nephew, Sam Gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at the Hidden Children's Conference in 1992 in Jerusalem, where all hidden children from all over the world met. And I got an honor to make HaMotzi over the challah. And to-- all the way to the left, with the blue shirt, you can see part of Abe [? Foxman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Who's here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Far left is my son-in-law, TJ Ruza. Next to him is my daughter Sandra. Next to her is my daughter-in-law, Susan and my son, Sam. And sitting is myself. In the middle as my first grandchild, Matthew Ruza, and my wife, Miryam Gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's here?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my two grandchildren from my son. The older one, which is almost three, is Max, and the little one is six-month-old Joshua.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Sandra, with her youngest-- her youngest child, which is Laurel Ruza, which you didn't see in the other picture.
